policy_statement: Provide 100% clean renewable energy through regional aggregation, City-property generation, and community-wide transition.
mechanism_description: Set and pursue a 100% clean renewable energy target by deploying/aggregating renewable supply, thereby displacing fossil-based electricity and decarbonizing the power mix.
primary_causal_pathway: Electricity supply policy → ↑ renewable electricity share → ↓ fossil generation/combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: The city/state/utility increases renewable electricity procurement and/or deployment (often via targets, aggregation, community ownership/access, and projects on public property). (2) Process: Added renewable generation (and associated contracting) supplies electricity with near-zero operational emissions, which displaces marginal fossil generation on the grid. (3) Outcome: Lower lifecycle and especially operational CO₂e emissions from the power sector, contributing to grid decarbonization and economy-wide emissions reductions (via cleaner electricity for buildings/transport).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced greenhouse gas emissions from electricity generation by replacing fossil fuel power with renewable electricity.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
(1) Dominant causal pathway: Increasing renewable electricity supply leads to reduced fossil electricity generation and thus reduced GHG emissions.  
(2) Why this primary over alternatives: While these policies can yield co-benefits (air quality, energy affordability, resilience), the core and largest climate effect across all instances is emissions mitigation through power-sector decarbonization.  
(3) Inherent causal chain for each secondary: None—resource-efficiency gains or adaptation/resilience benefits are not guaranteed outcomes of simply increasing renewable share (they depend on grid design, storage, reliability measures, and local hazard context).  
(4) Edge cases applied: If renewables are added without displacing fossil generation (e.g., high curtailment or parallel load growth), mitigation impact could be smaller, but the mechanism’s intended and typical pathway remains emissions reduction.  
(5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: Any policy under this mechanism is fundamentally about shifting the grid mix toward renewables/clean electricity; that is inherently a mitigation action regardless of ownership model or procurement approach.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a “renewable target” is met largely via unbundled RECs without inducing new renewable generation, or if added renewables are heavily curtailed, realized emissions reductions may be weaker; however, the mechanism classification remains Mitigation because the dominant intended pathway is grid decarbonization via fossil displacement.
key_indicators: “100% CLEAN RENEWABLE ENERGY”; “goal to achieve 100% clean, renewable energy community-wide by 2035”; “Installing renewable projects on city property”; “utilizing state funding”
co_benefits: Potential air quality and public health improvements from reduced fossil generation; potential energy cost stability and local green jobs (not specified as explicit objectives/mechanisms in the text).
instance_edge_case_notes: “Utilizing state funding” could also indicate an incentive-program element, but the core policy signal in the excerpt is the explicit 100% clean energy goal/target with associated deployment actions.
